narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 30, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plans to attend full-time and your lower-middle family income background. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants by predicting their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 56% chance that you would fail—high enough to tip the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes those predicted to succeed right away. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is well below the typical 5.53 average; this stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure, likely because consistently lower performance in later years often correlates with struggles on the intense bar exam. In contrast, your Year 1 decile of 4th, closer to the average of 5.71, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some early promise that the model appreciated. Your family income bracket also helped a bit, pulling the odds toward success compared to higher-income peers who sometimes face different pressures. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA are respectable, they weren't enough to offset the dominant concern from that low Year 3 ranking when stacked against typical students, who tend to maintain stronger deciles throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—many factors like determination and study habits play into bar success beyond these metrics. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year grades, perhaps aiming for a higher LSAT through targeted prep, or exploring part-time options if full-time work could build your resume differently. Schools often value persistence, so reapplying after addressing that Year 3 decile could shift the model's view dramatically. You're clearly motivated, and with focused improvements, doors like this one can reopen. Keep pushing—your background as a determined white male from a modest income level has already gotten you this far.
